description: Narrative-driven hip-hop with vivid storytelling, strong flow, and soulful beats
suno_tags: boom bap hip-hop, storytelling rap, soulful samples, male vocals, jazzy piano, raw, 90 BPM
vocal_style: Confident, rhythmic flow with natural cadence. Conversational delivery that pulls listeners into the story. Shifts between laid-back storytelling and intense emphasis on key bars. Authentic, not performative.

tips: Each verse should advance the story — beginning, middle, end across 3 verses
Use specific details: street names, times of day, weather, sounds
Internal rhymes and multisyllabic rhymes elevate the flow
Hook should capture the story's theme in 4 catchy lines
Bridge can be a reflective moment or spoken-word interlude

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Spoken Word]
Let me take you back to where it started
Corner of Fifth and Main, summer of '09

[Verse 1]
Fifteen years old with a notebook and a dream
Mama working doubles, nothing's what it seemed
Streetlights flickering, beats inside my skull
Writing rhymes on napkins till the pages were full
Old man at the corner store would let me freestyle
Said "Kid, you got something, stick around a while"
First open mic night, knees shaking on the stage
But the crowd went quiet and I turned the page

[Hook]
This is more than music, this is how I breathe
Every bar I write is everything I need
From the block to the booth, pen to the beat
This is my story, laid out on the street

[Verse 2]
Got a little older, city got a little colder
Friends went left, I kept the chip on my shoulder
Studio sessions running on borrowed time
Trading sleep for verses, trading doubt for rhymes
Label man came through, suit pressed and clean
Said "Sign right here, we'll make you a machine"
But I remembered what the old man told me
"Own your voice, kid, that's the only thing that's holy"

[Hook]
This is more than music, this is how I breathe
Every bar I write is everything I need
From the block to the booth, pen to the beat
This is my story, laid out on the street

[Bridge]
[Soft]
Sometimes I still drive past Fifth and Main
The corner store is gone but the lessons remain

[Verse 3]
Now I'm standing where I never thought I'd be
Same kid, same dream, just a wider scene
I record the truth, let the people decide
No ghost writers, just the voice inside
For every kid with a notebook in the dark
This verse is yours, this is where you start
The old man passed last winter, quiet and cold
But his words still echo in everything I hold

[Hook]
This is more than music, this is how I breathe
Every bar I write is everything I need
From the block to the booth, pen to the beat
This is my story, laid out on the street

[Outro]
[Spoken Word]
Fifth and Main. Always.
[End]
do: Tell a complete story with a clear arc across three verses
Use AABB rhyme scheme with internal rhymes for flow
Include specific sensory details — sights, sounds, smells of the setting
Let the hook be the moral or theme that ties the story together
Use spoken word for intro and outro to frame the narrative
dont: Don't write generic braggadocio — this is about storytelling
Don't break the narrative to add filler bars
Don't use complex words just to impress — clarity serves the story
Don't make the hook longer than 4 lines — it needs to be memorable
Don't lose the emotional thread — every verse should serve the arc
